g.data-narration: Welcome to Queues Using Dynamic Arrays. We'll explore how queues can grow automatically to handle unbounded data.
A queue is a First-In-First-Out structure. Elements enter at the rear and leave from the front, like a line at a store.
g.text: Elements added at rear, removed from front

g.data-narration: Fixed-size queues have three key problems: overflow when full, wasted space in linear queues, and fixed capacity chosen at compile time.
A circular queue wraps around like a ring, reusing empty slots. The array indices connect from end back to beginning.
The circular indexing formula is: next position equals current index plus one, modulo capacity. This handles wrap-around automatically.
The DynamicQueue structure has five fields: data pointer, front index, rear index, capacity, and size. The size field avoids wasting one slot.
g.text: typedef struct { int *data; int front; int rear; int capacity; int size; } DynamicQueue;

g.data-narration: The createQueue function allocates memory for the queue structure and its internal array, then initializes all fields to zero.
When the queue is full, resizeQueue doubles the capacity. Elements are copied in logical order from front to rear into the new linear array.
g.text: Copy in logical order (20,30,40,50), unwrap circular to linear starting at index 0

g.data-narration: Enqueue first checks if full and resizes if needed, then adds the element at rear position and advances rear using circular indexing.
Dequeue returns the front element, advances front using circular indexing, and decrements size. It also checks for underflow.
In this example, the queue has wrapped around (front=3, rear=2). When resizing, we must unwrap and copy elements in logical order: 20,30,40,50.
Enqueue is amortized O(1) because each element saves 2 credits during insertion. These credits pay for the O(n) copy during resize.
g.text: Accounting Method: Charge 3 units per enqueue

g.data-narration: Fixed-size queues are simple but can overflow. Dynamic queues grow automatically but have occasional O(n) resize costs.
To avoid holding excess memory, the queue can shrink when size drops below one-quarter capacity. The threshold avoids thrashing between sizes.
g.text: Why 1/4? Prevents thrashing - avoids repeated grow/shrink at boundary

g.data-narration: Dynamic array queues have key advantages: no overflow, cache-friendly contiguous memory, amortized O(1) operations, and random access capability.
Key exam points: remember the doubling strategy, explain amortized O(1), know the circular indexing formula, and understand the difference between size and capacity.
g.text: 🎯 Remember: Doubling strategy copies in logical order
🎯 Shrink at capacity/4 to avoid thrashing

g.data-narration: In this lecture, we covered dynamic queues that grow automatically using the doubling strategy, with amortized O(1) performance. Congratulations on completing this topic!
g.text: Dynamic arrays enable unbounded queues with amortized O(1) performance
